contract.title: SCOTUS rules Trump can fire FTC commissioners?
contract.resolution_criteria:
This market will resolve to 'Yes' if the U.S. Supreme Court rules in Trump v.
Slaughter (or a consolidated companion case) that the President may remove Federal
Trade Commission commissioners without cause, by December 31, 2026, 11:59 PM ET.
Resolution requires a majority opinion from the Supreme Court. Otherwise, this
market will resolve to 'No'.

Background: President Trump fired FTC Commissioners Rebecca Kelly Slaughter and
Alvaro Bedoya in early 2025. The case challenges the 1935 Humphrey's Executor
precedent, which prohibits presidents from removing commissioners of independent
agencies without cause. A ruling for Trump would fundamentally restructure the
FTC's independence from executive control and reshape antitrust enforcement authority.

The Supreme Court's term ends in June 2026; a decision is expected before then.
Market currently prices a 97% probability of YES.

settlement_entities.name: Supreme Court of the United States

conditions.label: Majority in Trump v. Slaughter
conditions.summary: By Dec 31, 2026, 11:59 PM ET, SCOTUS issues a majority opinion in Trump v. Slaughter holding the President may remove FTC commissioners without cause.

conditions.label: Majority in consolidated companion
conditions.summary: By Dec 31, 2026, 11:59 PM ET, SCOTUS issues a majority opinion in a case consolidated with Trump v. Slaughter holding the President may remove FTC commissioners without cause.

contract.narrative: Recent FTC activity—consumer guidance, workshops, and rulemakings—shows the agency operating normally and provides no signal on presidential removal power. None of the cited events materially affect odds that SCOTUS will permit at‑will firing of commissioners. The key determinant remains the pending Supreme Court case; a ruling is still outstanding.

timeline.title: FTC Chairman Ferguson Advises Companies to Comply with the Take It Down Act
timeline.regulator: Federal Trade Commission — Office of the Chairman
timeline.one_line_why: FTC chair advising compliance shows agency activity but gives no direct evidence that SCOTUS has ruled the President can remove FTC commissioners.

timeline.title: Protect your business from scams and online threats this National Small Business Week
timeline.one_line_why: FTC consumer guidance on scams is unrelated to the Supreme Court removal case and does not affect the chance of a pro‑Trump ruling.

timeline.title: FTC to Host Workshop on “Litigate the Fix” Merger Remedy Proposals
timeline.regulator: Federal Trade Commission — Bureau of Competition
timeline.one_line_why: FTC workshop on merger remedies is routine agency activity unrelated to the Supreme Court's removal‑power case; no material effect on YES odds.

timeline.title: Rule Concerning the Use of Prenotification Negative Option Plans
timeline.one_line_why: FTC issuing a rule shows the agency is operating but provides no meaningful evidence on whether SCOTUS will permit presidential removal of commissioners — neutral.

timeline.title: Rule on Unfair or Deceptive Rental Housing Fee Practices
timeline.one_line_why: FTC rule on rental-housing fees is routine agency action and doesn't materially inform whether SCOTUS will allow at‑will removal of FTC commissioners.
